title: Karma Yoga

principle.text:
The verse is precise in a way that is easy to misread. It does not say outcomes do not matter. It says you do not own them. The right to action, to show up, to prepare, to build the thing, is yours completely. The fruit, the response, the promotion, the approval, belongs to something larger than your wanting it.

For someone moving from CSM to PM, this territory is full of fruit-watching: who noticed, who responded, who saw the spec, whether the idea got picked up. The watching is not a problem. It is human. But when the watching becomes the fuel for the action, the action degrades. It starts optimising for visibility instead of value.

The practice is to locate the intrinsic signal. Not "did they like it" but "did I learn something true." Not "did the promotion come" but "did I do the work that would earn it if the system were fair." That signal is always available. The other one often is not.

move: Steel-man a take you disagree with. Pick one: "metrics matter more than process," or "CSMs rarely make good PMs," or "public frameworks are a crutch." Write 5 sentences arguing for it as if you believed it. Then write one sentence on what you might have been missing. Keep it private. Just sit with what surfaces.
question: What would you do differently this week if no one was watching?
